stories.headline: Fatal Hantavirus Case Confirmed in Douglas County Adult
stories.summary:
Colorado state and local health officials have confirmed a fatal case of hantavirus in a Douglas County adult, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment announced. The case is unrelated to the recent MV Hondius cruise ship outbreak that infected at least eight people and caused two confirmed deaths elsewhere.

Hantavirus infections caused by the Sin Nombre hantavirus occur regularly in Colorado, typically in spring and summer, and can cause severe and sometimes deadly respiratory disease. The deer mouse is the primary species responsible for human exposure in Colorado, through contact with rodent urine, feces, saliva, or nesting materials. Health officials said the infection appears to stem from local rodent exposure.

CDPHE said the risk to the general public remains low and the investigation is ongoing. Colorado has recorded 132 confirmed cases of Sin Nombre hantavirus since surveillance began in 1993, of which 47 have been fatal. The virus has not been shown to transmit person-to-person.

stories.headline: Longtime Denver County Judge Andre Rudolph Dies Unexpectedly at 59
stories.summary:
Denver County Court Judge Andre Rudolph, one of the court's longest-serving jurists, died unexpectedly on Friday, court officials confirmed Saturday. He was 59 years old. The cause of death was not immediately available, according to county court spokesperson Carolyn Tyler.

Rudolph was appointed to the bench in 2005 by then-Mayor John Hickenlooper, making him at age 37 one of the youngest judges in the state at the time. He began his legal career as a deputy state public defender in Colorado in 1993 and later served as a magistrate in both Denver District Court and the 17th Judicial District before his county court appointment.

Denver County Presiding Judge Kerri Lombardi remembered Rudolph as one of the most vibrant people she had ever known, praising his integrity, fairness, and deep community involvement. Rudolph was known for his work with Denver Kids, mentoring minority law students, and advocating for early prostate cancer detection. Funeral and memorial arrangements are pending.

stories.headline: Denver Restaurants Face Nation's Highest Inflation Rate as Closures Mount
stories.summary:
Denver's restaurant industry is under severe financial pressure, with menu prices rising an average of 28% since 2019 — 5.1 percentage points above the national average and the highest restaurant inflation rate in the country, according to the Denver Restaurant Liaison Project. Several well-known establishments have recently shuttered, including Wash Park Social and the longtime Imperial Chinese restaurant on South Broadway.

Wage costs have increased 50% since 2019, pushing many operators to reduce staff and shift to self-service ordering and payment systems to preserve thin margins. Declining alcohol sales, which have historically been a key profit driver, and reduced dining frequency among cost-conscious consumers are compounding the pressure. Restaurant reservation data shows a marked decline in bookings.

The stakes extend beyond individual businesses. Restaurants contribute 13% of Denver's sales tax revenue and account for 8.3% of local jobs, making the sector critical to the city's broader economy and tourism appeal. Operators also cite Denver's notoriously slow city permitting process as an additional obstacle to new openings and recovery.

stories.headline: Colorado Legislature Passes 'Artist Corporation' Law, Heads to Governor's Desk
stories.summary:
A first-of-its-kind bill creating a new legal business structure called an 'Artist Corporation,' or A-Corp, has cleared the Colorado General Assembly and is headed to Gov. Jared Polis for signature. The legislation establishes a formal framework recognizing artistic work as a legitimate professional enterprise under state law.

Advocates say the A-Corp designation will lend credibility to artists and performing arts professionals, making it easier for them to access health insurance and other benefits typically tied to traditional business structures. Boulder poet and performing artist Maggie Saunders told CPR News the law sends a broader signal that being an artist is a real job with merit worth pursuing.

Because Colorado allows individuals outside the state — and even outside the country — to form companies under its laws, supporters believe the A-Corp structure could have far-reaching effects beyond Colorado's borders. The bill's ultimate impact on health insurance access for artists, however, remains to be seen.

stories.headline: Denver Basic Income Project Research Featured at Colorado SunFest Panel
stories.summary:
Researchers and policy experts gathered at Colorado SunFest 2026, a daylong conference held in Denver, to examine the feasibility and impact of basic income programs, drawing in part on findings from the Denver Basic Income Project. The panel was hosted by the Scrivner Institute of Public Policy at the University of Denver's Josef Korbel School.

Discussions centered on what feasibility studies and program evaluations reveal about when and how cash dividend programs work, as well as the policy and funding challenges they face. The Denver Basic Income Project has been among the nation's most closely watched pilots, targeting individuals experiencing homelessness.

Panelists addressed the role these programs could play within broader social safety nets, including considerations around housing instability and income volatility. The event was part of a wider daylong conversation about issues shaping Colorado's future.

stories.headline: Distribution Industry Leaders Convene in Denver to Wrestle With AI Adoption Gaps
stories.summary:
Hundreds of distribution industry professionals gathered this week at The Inverness Denver hotel for the Shift | The Future of Distribution conference, where a central theme emerged: artificial intelligence investments have largely failed to meet expectations so far. A survey of 426 distribution companies by Modern Distribution Management found that while 73% expected at least a 2% margin improvement from AI, only 16% achieved that result.

One case study presented at the conference illustrated the pitfalls: Endries International automated a purchasing department workflow using AI, only to find months later that it had 'automated chaos' rather than solving root problems. Executives said the lesson was to understand and fix underlying processes before layering AI on top of them.

Researchers and panelists cautioned companies not to abandon AI investment but urged a focus on proven tools and serious change management. The National Association of Wholesaler-Distributors' vice president of research noted that companies achieving results started earlier and built organizational habits around the technology rather than simply purchasing software. The conference underscored Denver's growing role as a hub for national industry gatherings.

stories.headline: One Dead in Multi-Vehicle Crash on I-25 in Douglas County
stories.summary:
One person was killed and another was transported to a hospital following a crash involving three SUVs on Interstate 25 in Douglas County on Friday afternoon, according to Fox31 Denver. The crash forced a partial shutdown of the interstate.

Details about the cause of the crash, the identities of those involved, and the condition of the hospitalized individual were not immediately released. Colorado State Patrol was investigating the incident.

The stretch of I-25 through Douglas County is a heavily traveled commuter corridor connecting the Denver metro area to communities including Castle Rock and Colorado Springs. Crashes on this segment frequently cause significant traffic disruptions.

stories.headline: Antero Reservoir Expected to Empty Within Six Weeks Amid Historic Drought
stories.summary:
Denver Water expects to fully drain Antero Reservoir in Park County within approximately six weeks as the region grapples with historically low runoff from a drought-stricken snowpack, according to reporting by the Denver Gazette. The reservoir, located near Hartsel, is not yet empty but is approaching depletion.

The situation reflects the severity of Colorado's current water supply challenges heading into summer. Antero is one of several reservoirs in Denver Water's system and its depletion underscores the pressure drought conditions are placing on the region's water infrastructure.

Denver Water serves approximately 1.5 million people across the Denver metro area. Officials are monitoring conditions closely as runoff from the 2026 snowpack continues to underperform projections.

stories.headline: Avalanche Return to Ice for First Practice Ahead of Western Conference Final
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche returned to the ice Saturday for their first practice session in preparation for the Western Conference Final, after taking several days off following their second-round series victory. Head coach Jared Bednar noted the challenge of running a full practice with only five defensemen available.

The Avalanche advanced to the conference final after a dramatic comeback, rallying from a 3-0 deficit to defeat the Minnesota Wild in overtime — a result already reported in this digest. The team now awaits their next opponent as they pursue a return to Stanley Cup contention.

The thin defensive corps heading into practice reflects both injury concerns and roster management challenges the team must address before the series begins. The Avalanche are playing deep into the postseason for the first time in several years.

stories.headline: Rockies' Tanner Gordon Completes Grueling 6,134-Mile, Five-Flight Journey to Deliver Four Innings Against Pirates
stories.summary:
Colorado Rockies pitcher Tanner Gordon completed a two-day, five-flight, 6,134-mile odyssey across the country before taking the mound Thursday at PNC Park in Pittsburgh, where he delivered four innings of one-run ball with five strikeouts in a 7-2 Rockies loss. Gordon was summoned back to the major leagues on an emergency basis after starter Chase Dollander left the game with an elbow injury and reliever Jimmy Herget suffered a shoulder impingement.

Gordon had been sent down to Triple-A Albuquerque just one day earlier following Herget's activation from the injured list. His cross-country route took him from Pittsburgh to Albuquerque, then to Los Angeles, then on a red-eye to Atlanta, and finally back to Pittsburgh — landing at PNC Park with bloodshot eyes just hours before first pitch.

'When I saw him in the morning, his eyes were bloodshot,' Rockies manager Warren Schaeffer said. 'I asked him, are you ready to go? He said, yeah, a little coffee goes a long way.' Dollander was subsequently placed on the 15-day injured list with a right elbow sprain and is expected to miss a month or more, with Gordon the likely candidate to slot into the rotation.

stories.headline: Fossil Ridge's Addyson Smith Sweeps 5A 100 and 200 Titles at Colorado State Track Meet
stories.summary:
Fossil Ridge senior Addyson Smith captured both the Class 5A girls 100-meter and 200-meter state championships Saturday at Jeffco Stadium in Lakewood, closing out her prep career as Colorado's fastest female sprinter. Smith won the 100 in 11.60 seconds, defeating Heritage's Zona Welling — the same runner who edged Smith by one hundredth of a second in last year's final — and then took the 200 in 23.81.

Smith credited the narrow loss at last year's state meet as the fuel that drove her entire senior season. 'Every day when I wanted to just lie in my bed, I was like, no, this is the goal,' she said after being swarmed by teammates at the finish line. Smith is committed to run at Oregon State University next year.

Also at the meet, Niwot senior Addison Ritzenhein capped her high school career by setting a new 4A state record in the girls 1600 meters, clocking 4:44.47 to break the previous mark of 4:45.27 set by Katie Rainsberger in 2016. Ritzenhein, daughter of elite running coach Dathan Ritzenhein, finished her career with ten state track titles and three state cross-country titles.

joke.joke: I should've been a weatherman. You can be wrong every day and still keep your job.

topStory.headline: Pennsylvania House Primary Tests Whether Democrats Should Run on Economy or Anti-Trump Message
topStory.summary:
A Democratic primary in Pennsylvania's 7th Congressional District has emerged as a closely watched contest that reflects a broader debate inside the party about how to compete in the 2026 midterm elections. The race in the Lehigh Valley pits Bob Brooks, a retired firefighter and state union leader, against Ryan Crosswell, a Marine veteran and former federal prosecutor who resigned from the Justice Department's Public Integrity Section over an order to drop a corruption case against then-New York City Mayor Eric Adams.

Brooks has centered his campaign on economic populism, describing himself as a 'working-class candidate' and arguing that 'the whole system is rigged against us.' Crosswell has focused on what he calls the lawlessness of the Trump administration, framing his Justice Department resignation as 'a real No Kings moment' and arguing that corruption is a kitchen-table issue that resonates with voters.

Despite the ideological range between the two candidates, party leaders across the Democratic spectrum have lined up behind Brooks. Sen. Bernie Sanders of Vermont and Pennsylvania Gov. Josh Shapiro are among those backing the retired firefighter, who would be one of the few House members without a college degree if elected. Democratic operatives say they view Brooks as better positioned to connect with working-class voters on economic concerns, which they expect to dominate the 2026 general election landscape.

The consolidation of establishment and progressive support behind Brooks represents what some Democratic strategists describe as an acknowledgment that the party needs candidates with broader working-class appeal and fewer candidates drawn from legal and professional backgrounds. Andrew Mamo, a spokesperson for The Bench, a Democratic group supporting Brooks, said the backing is 'an admission that we need more normal people and fewer lawyers.' The district is considered one of Pennsylvania's most competitive congressional seats, making the outcome of the primary a potential indicator of the party's strategic direction heading into the midterms.
